He participated in anti-German underground activities, was detained in July 1918, and sentenced by a German military field court to two years' imprisonment. After the Ukrainian State fell in December 1918, he was released. From April 1919, he worked in the Ministry of Internal Affairs of the Ukrainian People's Republic, later transferred to the General Staff of the Ukrainian People's Army, where, from May 1920, he headed the Information Bureau (intelligence and counterintelligence service). After evacuation to Poland, he briefly worked in the Second Department (intelligence and counterintelligence) of the Polish General Staff, returning to his previous position in November 1921. His further fate is unknown.

Public reaction
The Beilis case marked the culmination of the persecution of the Jewish community of the Russian Empire in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, when Jews were scapegoated for the challenges and problems the Russian Empire was facing at the time. The trial remains notable for the significant public resonance it generated in the press, both left- and right-wing. The majority of Russian and Ukrainian public figures opposed the new blood libel.
On December 13, 1911, a protest titled To Russian Society (Regarding the Blood Libel Against Jews) was published, drafted by Vladimir Korolenko and signed by 82 prominent writers, scholars, and public figures. Among the signatories, besides Korolenko himself, were Zinaida Gippius, Dmitry Merezhkovsky, Alexander Blok, Maxim Gorky, Fyodor Sologub, Leonid Andreyev, and Vyacheslav Ivanov. The appeal noted that the "blood libel" was originally leveled against early Christians and cited, in particular, Greek Patriarch Gregory, who called the legend of Jews using Christian blood a "repulsive prejudice of those weak in faith".
The campaign against the Beilis case had a powerful international dimension. In March 1912, a protest signed by 206 German intellectuals, including Thomas Mann, Gerhart Hauptmann, and Werner Sombart, emerged in Germany. This was followed by a protest from 240 British public figures, signed by the entire church leadership, led by the Archbishop of Canterbury Randall Thomas Davidson, the Speaker of the House of Commons, former president of the Royal Academy of Arts Edward John Poynter, H.G. Wells, Oliver Joseph Lodge, Austen Chamberlain, Arthur James Balfour, James George Frazer, Thomas Hardy, and others. A French protest, gathering 150 signatures, included Anatole France and Octave Mirbeau.
Among Beilis' defenders were prominent Russian nationalists and antisemites, such as the editors of the newspaper Kievlyanin, Dmitry Pikhno and, after his death, Vasily Shulgin. On May 30, 1912, Pikhno published Krasovsky's exposés under the headline "You yourselves offer human sacrifices!" which immediately became a topic of discussion in the Duma. From the first day of the trial, Shulgin began publishing a series of articles in Kievlyanin sharply criticizing the prosecution:

You don't need to be a lawyer, just a sensible person, to understand that the accusation against Beilis is nonsense that any defense attorney could easily dismantle. One cannot help but feel ashamed for the Kiev prosecutor's office and for all of Russian justice, which dared to face the judgment of the entire world with such feeble evidence.
Shulgin's opinion carried significant weight. As Maurice Samuel writes, "coming from a well-known monarchist and antisemite, this article reverberated like a thunderclap across Russia, with its echo resonating in the Western world". Mikhail Menshikov acknowledged that Shulgin, "with a few newspaper lines, ground the two-year work of the Kiev prosecutor's office, approved by the highest judicial authorities, into dust".
The government countered critics of the trial with repressive measures. According to various sources, between 66 and 102 cases of press repression were recorded during and in connection with the trial. According to Leon Trotsky, 34 fines totaling 10,400 rubles were imposed, 30 publications were confiscated, four editors were arrested, and two newspapers were shut down before the trial. Aleksandr Tager claims that six editors were arrested, eight were prosecuted, 36 newspapers were confiscated, three were closed, and 43 fines totaling 12,850 rubles were issued.
Even the monarchist Kievlyanin faced repression: the September 27 issue, with Shulgin's editorial accusing Chaplinsky of intimidating subordinates and stifling attempts to fully investigate the case in favor of the Black Hundreds, was confiscated. In 1914, Shulgin was sentenced to three months in prison for libel over this accusation but was not arrested due to his status as a Duma deputy, and the sentence was soon overturned by the emperor. However, on October 25, 1916, the Criminal Cassation Department of the Senate, with Chaplinsky's participation "to the general shame and contrary to longstanding senatorial practice," upheld the guilty verdict against Shulgin. Maklakov was also sentenced to three months in prison for articles stating that the jury's verdict saved the court's good name, as were the editors of the newspapers Russkie Vedomosti and Russkaya Mysl who published his articles; however, the sentence was not enforced, as the judicial chamber deliberately delayed the cassation appeal until the revolution. Between 1913 and 1915, 25 lawyers from the St. Petersburg Judicial Chamber faced prosecution for their collective statement condemning the trial.
The trial was actively satirized in literature, including in the magazines Satirikon and Novyi Satirikon. The former published a poem by Vsevolod Knyazev, Beilisiada, whose title quickly became a household term; the latter dedicated a special issue to the trial, which was confiscated by the authorities. It included, among other things, a "Complete Dictionary of Words" by Foma Opiskin (pseudonym of Arkady Averchenko), with entries such as:

A – Acquittal – a word savored with particular relish by semi-literate right-wing newspapers. As is known, their expectations were not met.
In protest against the Beilis case, strikes, student gatherings, and other similar events were organized; in the event of a guilty verdict, a general strike was planned in St. Petersburg. At the same time, the Black Hundred press continued its antisemitic campaign. The newspaper Zemshchina, the organ of the Union of the Russian People, wrote:

Dear, poor children, beware and steer clear of your ancient enemy, tormentor, and child-killer, cursed by God and men – the Jew! As soon as you see his demonic face or smell his Jewish odor, run away from him as if from a plague.
The newspaper Russkoe Znamya openly called for the genocide of Jews:

The government must recognize Jews as a so dangerous people to the humanity as wolves, scorpions, vipers, venomous spiders, and other creatures subject to extermination for their predation on humans, whose destruction is encouraged by law... Jews must be placed in conditions where they continuously die out: this is now the duty of the government and the country's best people.
Publications by the Black Hundred press, fueling antisemitic hysteria, acted like a viral infection, provoking mass phobias and even demands for physical violence against Jews.
Public tension peaked on November 10, 1913, the final day of the trial. Members of the monarchist organization "Double-Headed Eagle" held a memorial service for Yushchinskyi in Saint Sophia Cathedral, located opposite the courthouse. There were fears of an impending pogrom. The authorities took extraordinary security measures, with police posting guards throughout Kiev. The acquittal verdict dispelled all fears and concerns.

Among prominent Russian intellectuals, Vasily Rozanov supported the "ritual" version (for political reasons, believing that a "Jewish yoke" was descending on Russia that needed to be stopped), publishing a series of articles in the newspaper Novoye Vremya titled "The Olfactory and Tactile Relationship of Jews to Blood" (for which he was expelled from the St. Petersburg Religious-Philosophical Society). Influenced by Rozanov's ideas, Pavel Florensky also supported this view.
Florensky, convinced of the existence of Jewish ritual murders, sent Rozanov two articles for anonymous publication: Prof. D.A. Khvolson on Ritual Murders and Jews and the Fate of Christians. Rozanov included both in his book "The Olfactory and Tactile Relationship of Jews to Blood" as appendices. Professor Aleksandr Bronzov of the St. Petersburg Theological Academy sharply criticized Korolenko's protest in the journal Tserkovny Vestnik.
To this day, the jury's affirmative response to the first court question, which combined the fact of the murder, its location, and its nature, provides grounds for blood libel proponents to claim that the ritual murder was proven in court.
Aleksandr Tager wrote that "the entire country, except the far right, was against the trial: not only the revolutionary underground but also the moderate and liberal opposition, to varying degrees, opposed the government in connection with the trial". Public figures across the political spectrum spoke of the opposition to the Beilis trial as a direct confrontation with the authorities.
Russian nationalist Mikhail Menshikov criticized Shulgin for his articles:

It would be better for a serious publication with the traditions of Kievlyanin not to make baseless accusations against the authorities, especially at a time when they are under siege by forces hostile to Russia. By attacking the prosecutor's office, isn't Mr. Shulgin joining the Jewish terror? (...) Mr. Shulgin knows well that the investigation was conducted under the supervision not only of the Kiev prosecutor but also of the highest judicial authorities. (...) Is it proper, in such a case, to publicly discredit such a seriously constructed accusation before the trial?
The same idea, though expressed differently, was voiced by social democrat Leon Trotsky:

Since the gang of "ritualists", starting with the boy's murderers, Kiev thieves, and continuing with the police and judicial authorities, is crowned by the Tsar of All Russia, the agitation against the blood libel, regardless of the intentions of liberal politicians and editors, took on a distinctly revolutionary, anti-monarchist character.

Outcome of the case
The acquittal of Beilis was evident, but the trial failed to resolve the question of who actually murdered Yushchinskyi. Despite widespread belief in the guilt of Vera Cheberyak and her accomplice thieves, this version was not properly investigated. The head of the Moscow detective police, Arkady Koshko, wrote in his memoirs that the establishment of the truth was hindered by public uproar and several unprofessional actions by the investigation. The actions of lawyers from the Jewish community, as well as bribery and threats against witnesses, contributed to the confusion. Other versions, beyond the ritual murder and gang revenge theories, such as a killing by a maniac, were simply not considered. Just a few days after the trial ended, another attempt to stage a ritual murder occurred in Kiev, known as the Fastov Case, but it was relatively quickly debunked.
Leon Trotsky described the trial's outcome as the "moral bankruptcy" of the government. "The Beilis trial is a police Tsushima, which will never be forgiven", wrote police official Lyubimov, tasked with analyzing the trial's progress. Following Lyubimov, both Aleksandr Tager and Aleksandr Solzhenitsyn referred to the trial as a "judicial Tsushima".
Particularly telling were the blatant attempts by the prosecution and authorities, both during and outside the trial, to shield Cheberyak and her accomplices, effectively demonstrating the government's complicity with criminals. "The two-story house [of the Cheberyaks] on Yurkovskaya [Street] has been placed under protection, and doubting its trustworthiness has become outright dangerous. (...) it's as if an invisible patriotic banner is hovering over the house," wrote Korolenko. In Trotsky's ironic words, the police "fined and confiscated newspapers for exposing Cheberyak's gang of thieves with such zeal, as if it were a direct insult to His Majesty".
Beilis' acquittal significantly calmed public opinion, as an innocent man was not convicted. Korolenko wrote of his impression of the trial's outcome: "The joy was immense (...) Such collective joy, such a flood of joy, that it completely overshadowed the impression of the thousand Black Hundreds gathered as a dark stain at Saint Sophia Cathedral... I'm glad I saw it with my own eyes".

The Beilis trial was held in the year when the Romanov dynasty celebrated 300 years of rule over Russia. With this trial, tsarism created a fitting historical monument for itself, vividly showcasing a gallery of figures, rising gradually from a thieves' den to the foot of the throne – from Cheberyak to the Tsar.
According to Viktor Chernov, the failure of the prosecution in the Beilis trial "belongs to the brilliant pages of the Russian public's struggle against the plague of antisemitism". Historian Shmuel Ettinger writes that "broad progressive circles" saw in the acquittal "a clear defeat of the authorities and a victory for liberal and radical public opinion". A similar assessment is found in the Shorter Jewish Encyclopedia.

Historiography
The Beilis case generated a substantial body of literature. Between 1911 and 1913 alone (during the investigation and trial), over 80 books and pamphlets were published as standalone editions.
The primary historical document on the Beilis case is the three-volume stenographic record of the trial proceedings. It was first published in Kiev in 1913 at the initiative of the newspaper Kievskaya Mysl and was reissued in Moscow in 2006 in an abridged version by the publishing house Russkaya Ideya.
Following the Russian Revolution and the opening of tsarist archives, numerous previously classified documents were made public, shedding light on the biased and illegal actions of the authorities in the Beilis case. Some materials from the interrogations and testimonies of the Extraordinary Investigative Commission of the Provisional Government were included in the seven-volume publication Fall of the Tsarist Regime, which significantly influenced public opinion on the Beilis case. These documents further supported the theory that Vera Cheberyak and her accomplice thieves were the true culprits. The most comprehensive work dispelling any remaining doubts about Beilis' innocence and the fabricated nature of the ritual murder accusation was the seminal monograph by Soviet lawyer Aleksandr Semyonovich Tager, published in 1933 to mark the 20th anniversary of the trial. It was republished in 1934 and 1995.
Public interest in the Beilis case was reignited in the 1960s with the release of American writer Bernard Malamud's bestselling novel The Fixer and Maurice Samuel's study Blood Libel: The Strange History of the Beilis Case. However, in Soviet publications after 1934, the Beilis case was rarely mentioned, except for a brief entry in the Great Soviet Encyclopedia.
Research resumed in the 1990s after the dissolution of the USSR. On October 28–29, 1993, an international academic conference was held in Kyiv to commemorate the 80th anniversary of the trial's conclusion. In the same year, a book titled The Beilis Case was published by psychiatrist Mikhail Buyanov, with one chapter dedicated to a detailed analysis of the psychiatric examination.
In 1999, all discovered materials from the Extraordinary Investigative Commission of the Provisional Government were published with a foreword by Genry Reznik. Although only the first part of the three-volume investigative file of the Commission was found in state archives, the Commission's chairman, Nikolay Muravyov, had copied all the case materials, and these documents were preserved.
The Beilis case continues to be studied in the 21st century. In 2006, Leonid Katsis published a 500-page book, Blood Libel and Russian Thought: A Historical-Theological Study of the Beilis Case (Russian: Кровавый навет и русская мысль: Историко-теологическое исследование дела Бейлиса), and in 2007, Andris Grutups offered a legal perspective in Beilisiada: The Case of Mendel Beilis' Accusation of Ritual Murder (translated from Latvian).
The topic is also addressed in numerous studies, such as Semyon Reznik's Corruption by Hatred (2001), Sergey Stepanov's The Black Hundred in Russia (Russian: Чёрная сотня в России) (2005), Lev Lurye's 22 Deaths, 63 Versions (Russian: 22 смерти, 63 версии) (2011), and others.

In culture
The Beilis case inspired the major Jewish prose writer of the early 20th century, Sholem Aleichem, to write the novel Blood Jest. The work was completed on January 7, 1913, before the trial began. During the Beilis trial, Fyodor Sologub wrote The Eerie Lullaby, in which he condemned both the true murderers of the child and the trial's organizers, using such objectivist tones that readers could interpret the work as either a justification of the murder or a vile provocation.
The first documentary film, based on the private investigations of I. Krasovsky and S. Brazul-Brushkovsky, was shot by cinematographer Vladimir Dobrzhansky for the Kiev cinema Express owned by Yu. Shantser. The film was shown in August 1912 at private screenings and abroad. Another documentary was produced by the Pathé Brothers' company. Released in 1913, it was banned in Russia.
Concurrently with the documentaries, during the trial, director Iosif Soyfer began filming a feature film, Mysteries of Kyiv, or the Beilis Trial. The film was sold abroad and screened in various countries in Western Europe and the United States.
After the revolution, in 1917, Nikolay Breshko-Breshkovsky directed another feature film, Vera Cheberyak.
In 1968, the feature film The Fixer based on Bernard Malamud's novel of the same name, dedicated to the Beilis case, was released in the United States.
In 1993, director Grigory Ilugdin produced the documentary The Long Night of Menachem Beilis. In 2003, director Oleksandr Muratov produced the documentary Arnold Margolin – An Outstanding Ukrainian and Jew.
The theme was also reflected in late 20th-century fiction. In addition to The Fixer, other novels addressed the Beilis case, such as Gely Ryabov's The Pale Horse of the Jew Beilis.
